hook.text: Hawaii sits over a hotspot—a plume of magma rising from deep in Earth's mantle.

buildup.text: The Pacific plate slides over the hotspot, creating a chain of islands one by one.

discovery.text: The oldest Hawaiian island is furthest from the hotspot. The youngest is still erupting.

twist.text: A new island called Lōʻihi is forming underwater and will surface in thousands of years.

climax.text: The Hawaiian chain stretches 6,000 km, recording 80 million years of plate movement.

punchline.text: The plate moves. The hotspot stays. Islands line up.

quiz.question: Why do the Hawaiian Islands form a chain?
options: The plate moves over a stationary hotspot
Earthquakes split one island into many
Ocean currents push volcanic rock in a line
